translatedText: "A Vindication of the Rights of Men" (1790)

As a rebuttal to Edmund Burke's "Reflections on the Revolution in France", it is an early political and feminist text that attacks aristocracy, advocates republicanism, and criticizes Burke's gender-biased language.
"An Historical and Moral View of the French Revolution" (1794)

A book that summarizes the early history of the French Revolution, counters the anti-revolutionary mood in Britain, and argues that the revolution was the result of a social, economic, and political crisis.
"Letters Written in Sweden, Norway, and Denmark" (1796)

A travelogue containing reflections on Scandinavian society and figures, questions of identity, and her relationship with Imlay. It explores the value of subjective experience and imagination, advocating for women's liberation and education.
Pedagogical Writings

Through "Thoughts on the Education of Daughters" (1787) and her children's book "Original Stories from Real Life" (1788), she argued that children should be taught how to think and be educated to cultivate a middle-class ethos.
What is Mary Wollstonecraft's most famous book?
Her most famous book is "A Vindication of the Rights of Woman", published in 1792.
What did Mary Wollstonecraft argue about women's education?
She argued that women are not innately inferior to men, but only appear so due to a lack of education, and therefore they should receive a quality education.
Is there any famous figure among Mary Wollstonecraft's daughters?
Yes, her second daughter, Mary Wollstonecraft Godwin, later married Percy Bysshe Shelley and became Mary Shelley, the author of the novel "Frankenstein".
Which philosophers did Mary Wollstonecraft interact with?
She interacted with radical writer Thomas Paine, philosopher William Godwin, and others, and eventually married William Godwin.
What was Mary Wollstonecraft's stance on the French Revolution?
She condemned the Jacobin regime and the Reign of Terror, but at the same time maintained the position that the revolution was a great achievement.
Born as the third son of Estêvão da Gama in Sines, Portugal
Around 1480
Joined the Order of Santiago, headed by Crown Prince John (later King John II)
Dispatched to the Algarve under the orders of John II to resolve a dispute with France
July 8, 1497
Departed from Belém, Lisbon for the first expedition to India
March 2, 1498
Began a stay on an island near Mozambique
April 7, 1498
Arrived in Mombasa
April 14, 1498
Arrived in Malindi
April 24, 1498
Left Malindi with a new navigator and headed for India
Arrived in Calicut, India
Late August 1498
Left Calicut and began the journey home
August 29, 1498
Departed from India
January 7, 1499
Arrived in Malindi
September 9, 1499
Returned to Lisbon
February 12, 1502
Commanded the vanguard of the second expedition to India and departed
June 1502
Arrived in Mozambique, established a trading post, and signed a trade agreement with Sofala
August 20, 1502
Arrived at Anjediva Island near Goa, India
September 29, 1502
Captured the Islamic pilgrim ship Miri and committed a massacre
October 18, 1502
Moved to Kannur to negotiate trade
October 29, 1502
Arrived off the coast of Calicut
November 1, 1502
Fired indiscriminate artillery fire at the port of Calicut
Early 1503
Departed from India with a fleet loaded with spices and started the journey back
Around September 1503
Arrived in Lisbon
Appointed Viceroy of India, left Lisbon in April and arrived in India in September (3rd voyage)
Awarded the title of Count of Vidigueira by King Manuel I for his contributions in pioneering the new route
Appointed as a policy advisor by John III
December 24, 1524
Died of malaria in Cochin, India
Pioneered the Route to India
He pioneered the route to India, which had been Portugal's long-cherished desire since Bartolomeu Dias discovered the Cape of Good Hope.
First European to Establish a Sea Route to India
He was the first European to sail to India across the Atlantic Ocean and the southern coast of Africa.
Awarded the title of Count of Vidigueira
In recognition of his contribution to pioneering the new route, he was awarded the title of Count of Vidigueira by King Manuel I in 1519.
Three Voyages to India in Total
During his lifetime, he sailed to India three times in total.
Served as Viceroy of India
He was appointed Viceroy of India in 1514 and served as Viceroy until his death in 1524.
When was Vasco da Gama born?
Vasco da Gama was born in 1469 in Sines, Alentejo province, in southwestern Portugal.
What are Vasco da Gama's main achievements?
Vasco da Gama became the first European to sail across the Atlantic Ocean and along the southern coast of Africa to India, pioneering the route to India.
How many times did Vasco da Gama sail to India?
Vasco da Gama sailed to India a total of three times during his lifetime.
Where did Vasco da Gama die?
Vasco da Gama died of malaria in Cochin, India, on December 24, 1524.
How long did Vasco da Gama's first voyage to India take?
Vasco da Gama's first voyage to India took a total of two years from his departure from Lisbon in July 1497 until his return.
195 BC
Born
139 BC
Volunteered as an envoy to form an alliance with Dayuan against the Xiongnu for the Han Dynasty and departed for the Western Regions
126 BC
Returned to the Han Dynasty
123 BC
Contributed to Emperor Wu's attack on the Xiongnu and was appointed as Weiwei (Commandant of Guards) and Bowanghou (Marquis of Bowang)
121 BC
Was demoted to a commoner after being held responsible for missing a military deadline
119 BC
Dispatched as an envoy to Wusun
114 BC
Died
Pioneered the Silk Road
Made a significant contribution to the pioneering of the Silk Road.
Conveyed Knowledge of the Western Regions
Contributed to the establishment of new strategies by conveying valuable knowledge about the Western Regions to the Han Dynasty.
Contributed to the Attack on the Xiongnu
Made a great contribution by utilizing his experience and geographical knowledge during Emperor Wu's attack on the Xiongnu in 123 BC.
Proposed Alliance with Wusun
Laid the foundation for improving Han-Wusun relations by proposing a marriage alliance and eastward migration with Wusun.
Who is Zhang Qian?
Zhang Qian was a traveler, diplomat, and explorer of the Han Dynasty in 2nd century BC China, who made a significant contribution to the pioneering of the Silk Road.
What was the main purpose of Zhang Qian's dispatch to the Western Regions?
The Han Dynasty recruited an envoy to form an alliance with Dayuan to counter the Xiongnu, and Zhang Qian volunteered and headed for Dayuan.
What was the greatest achievement that Zhang Qian's exploration of the Western Regions brought to the Han Dynasty?
Although the alliance was not established, the valuable knowledge about the Western Regions brought back by Zhang Qian greatly contributed to the Han Dynasty's formulation of new strategies.
What are the items said to have been brought from the Western Regions by Zhang Qian?
There is a legend that he brought back items such as grapes, pomegranates, and peaches from the Western Regions.
Born as the eldest son of Pepin the Short and Bertrada of Laon in Herstal, Wallonia, in the Frankish Kingdom near Liège, Belgium.
His father, Pepin the Short, became the King of the Franks with the approval of Pope Zachary.
Pope Stephen II conferred the royal title on Pepin and his sons Charles and Carloman.
September 24, 768
After the death of his father Pepin the Short, he succeeded to the throne and took over Austrasia and the northern regions.
Became the sole ruler after the death of his brother Carloman.
Began attacking the Saxons.
Conquered and annexed the Lombard Kingdom in northern Italy.
April 774
Attended the Easter festival in Rome with the Pope and reaffirmed the promise to transfer the Papal States.
August 15, 778
Suffered a crushing defeat from Basque attacks at the Battle of Roncevaux Pass during his expedition to the Iberian Peninsula.
Visited Rome and had his two sons, Pepin and Louis, consecrated and bestowed upon them the thrones of Lombardy and Aquitaine, respectively.
His wife Hildegard and his mother Bertrada of Laon died consecutively, and he remarried Fastrada.
Conquered Thuringia and Bavaria, and deposed Tassilo III, Duke of Bavaria.
Greatly defeated the Avars along the Danube River and drove them out of his borders.
Built a palace and a royal chapel in Aachen, northwestern Germany, making it his de facto capital.
May 799
Pope Leo III was attacked by his opponents and fled to Charlemagne's court to request assistance.
